primary_factors: No plowable snow or freezing precipitation signal in the provided forecast window; no active weather alerts
Wind chills are nowhere near Michigan extreme-cold closure thresholds (no credible source shows ≤ -20°F for bus hours)
Main credible risk is localized refreeze/black ice from leftover moisture, most likely on bridges/overpasses, shaded rural connectors, and parking lots
Infrastructure status indicates roads broadly clear/normal operations; any response would be spot anti-icing rather than plowing
News intelligence shows no neighboring-district closure domino signals and no community buzz suggesting hazardous widespread conditions
decision_rationale: All specialists converge on a low-closure outcome. Meteorology and web verification show a dry pattern in the provided lead-in data (Sun 3/8) with no snow accumulation expected and wind chills in the mid-20s°F range—well above the -15°F to -20°F and ≤-20°F closure thresholds. Infrastructure monitoring indicates roads are already clear and no storm-response plowing is expected; the operational concern shifts to isolated refreeze/black ice if any standing water from the prior wet period remains and pavement temps briefly hit/breach freezing pre-dawn Monday. Safety analysis flags this as a moderate, commute-window risk, but this type of patchy hazard in West Michigan typically leads to spot treatment and (at most) a short delay rather than a full closure, unless glazing becomes widespread across secondary roads. News intel found no confirmed neighboring-district closures/delays and no meaningful community chatter pointing to deteriorating roads, which further supports staying open. Key uncertainty: we lack a clean, directly provided hourly forecast for Mon 3/9 6–8 AM, so the probability is not driven to ~0%; it remains a small non-zero tail risk for a surprise widespread glaze.

collaboration.summary: Agents reached consensus after 1 round(s) with 1.0% spread. Average probability: 5%.
